story_titles: Climate.gov was destroyed. Open data saved it
Apple's new SpeechAnalyzer API, benchmarked against Whisper and its predecessor
Linux 0.11 rewritten in idiomatic Rust, boots in QEMU
full_text: Climate.gov was destroyed, but open data saved it with over 15 years of key climate information preserved. So back in 2020, the Trump Administration made some significant cuts to NOAA's funding, and that ultimately resulted in Climate.gov shutting down. After Climate.gov went down, a group of former NOAA employees - including Rebecca Lindsey, Anna Eshelman, and Mary Lindsey - teamed up to rebuild the site, and they ended up creating a new platform called Climate.us. This preservation of climate data is crucial, as it ensures that vital information remains accessible for research and decision-making. Imagine your device could transcribe speech more accurately than ever before - what would that even be like, having something that can pick up on every word so clearly. Apple's new SpeechAnalyzer API has been benchmarked against Whisper models, showing it to be the most accurate on-device speech engine, with a word error rate of 2.12% on clean speech. This means that SpeechAnalyzer can transcribe audio roughly three times faster than Whisper Small, making it a significant improvement for on-device speech recognition. What if you could run a Linux kernel written in modern Rust, with stronger types and clearer module boundaries - that's a pretty interesting idea to consider, and it could change things. The Linux 0.11 kernel has been rewritten in idiomatic Rust, allowing it to boot on emulated i386 hardware and run a full init shell coreutils stack. This Rust rewrite preserves the original kernel's semantics while rethinking its expression, making it an interesting project for developers and researchers. Follow for daily tech news
